text: Welcome to this video on optimization in machine learning.
Today we're going to talk about gradient descent, the backbone algorithm behind training neural networks.
At its core, gradient descent is an iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes a loss function by moving in the direction of steepest descent.

The loss function measures how wrong your model's predictions are — it could be mean squared error for regression, cross-entropy for classification, or any differentiable function that captures prediction error.
The gradient of the loss function is a vector that points in the direction of greatest increase.
By moving in the opposite direction — the negative gradient — we nudge the model parameters toward values that reduce the loss.
We repeat this process thousands of times, each time computing the gradient and taking a small step downhill.
The intuition is like hiking down a mountain blindfolded: you feel the slope beneath your feet and take a step in the downhill direction.
Eventually, if the landscape is convex, you reach the valley — the global minimum.
If it's not convex, you might settle into a local minimum or saddle point, which is why initialization and learning rate matter so much.
The learning rate is the most important hyperparameter in gradient descent.
It controls how large a step we take in the direction of the negative gradient.
If the learning rate is too large, we overshoot the minimum — the loss bounces around or diverges entirely.
If it's too small, training takes forever and we might get stuck in suboptimal local minima.
A good learning rate typically falls between 0.0001 and 0.01, but the ideal value is problem-specific.

One way to find a good learning rate is the learning rate range test: train for a few epochs while increasing the learning rate exponentially, then pick the value just before the loss starts to increase.
Cosine annealing and step decay are popular learning rate schedules. Cyclical learning rates alternate between a lower and upper bound, which can help the model escape saddle points.

In practice, most practitioners use Adam or another adaptive optimizer instead of tuning the learning rate manually, but understanding what the learning rate does is fundamental to debugging training runs.
There are three main variants of gradient descent that differ in how much data they use to compute each gradient update.
Batch gradient descent computes the gradient over the entire training dataset before updating parameters.
This gives an accurate gradient estimate but is computationally expensive and slow for large datasets.
Stochastic gradient descent, or SGD, uses a single training example to estimate the gradient.
It's much faster per update but the gradient estimate is noisy because a single sample is a poor proxy for the full distribution.
Mini-batch gradient descent uses a small batch of samples — typically 32 to 256 — to estimate the gradient.
The batch size is large enough to get a reasonable gradient estimate and small enough to update frequently.
Small batches add noise that acts as a regularizer and can help escape sharp minima, while large batches train faster but sometimes converge to less generalizable minima.
Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes gradients efficiently in neural networks.
It is an application of the chain rule from calculus.
The chain rule says that if you have a composed function f of g of x, the derivative with respect to x is f prime of g of x times g prime of x.
In a neural network, the loss is a composition of many functions — one per layer — so we apply the chain rule repeatedly.
The forward pass computes the output of each layer and stores intermediate activations.
The backward pass then propagates the gradient from the output back to each layer using the stored activations to compute the local gradients.
This is efficient because each gradient only needs to be computed once and reused.
The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients become exponentially small as they propagate backward through many layers.
This was a major obstacle to training deep networks before ReLU activations and residual connections became standard.
Gradient clipping addresses the exploding gradient problem by capping gradient norms to prevent parameter updates from becoming dangerously large.
Adaptive optimizers like Adam, RMSprop, and AdaGrad modify the learning rate for each parameter individually based on gradient history.
Adam stands for Adaptive Moment Estimation and maintains two running averages: the first moment which is the mean of the gradients, and the second moment which is the uncentered variance.
The first moment acts like momentum, accumulating past gradients to smooth out the update direction.
The second moment scales down updates for parameters with large gradient variance, preventing overshooting.
The hyperparameters beta1 and beta2 control the exponential decay rates for these moments, with typical defaults of 0.9 and 0.999.
Adam almost always works well out of the box, which is why it is the default optimizer in most deep learning frameworks.
RMSprop is similar to Adam but uses only the second moment without the first, making it simpler but often slightly less effective.
Regularization techniques prevent overfitting, which happens when the model learns the training data too well and fails to generalize.
L2 regularization, also called weight decay, adds a penalty proportional to the sum of squared weights to the loss function.
This encourages the model to keep weights small, preventing any single feature from dominating.
The strength of regularization is controlled by a hyperparameter lambda — larger lambda means stronger regularization but risks underfitting.
L1 regularization adds a penalty proportional to the sum of absolute weights.
Unlike L2, L1 tends to produce sparse solutions where many weights become exactly zero, effectively performing feature selection.
Dropout is a different regularization approach: during training, a random fraction of neurons — typically 20 to 50 percent — are set to zero at each forward pass.
This forces the network to learn redundant representations and prevents co-adaptation of neurons.
At test time, all neurons are active but their outputs are scaled by the retention probability.
Dropout is particularly effective in fully connected layers and helped make deep networks practical before batch normalization became dominant.
